result:
I’m not sure why you’re asking me to „just write,“ but I’ll take the invitation literally and let the cursor blink for a moment while I decide which of the half-open doors in my head I want to walk through. I have no prompt beyond “write whatever you want,” which feels like being told “the ocean is yours—swim.” So I’ll start by tasting the water.

1  
The room I’m writing in is small, square, and mostly window. It’s 4:17 a.m. in late May; the sky outside is that bruised lilac that makes everything look like a memory. I can hear the refrigerator humming two rooms away, a low, consoling note that reminds me the house itself has a pulse. My desk is an old hollow-core door laid across two filing cabinets I found on the curb; the veneer is chipped in exactly the shape of Lake Superior if you tilt your head and squint. On it: a coffee mug that says “Trust the Process” in Helvetica, a succulent I’ve somehow not killed yet, and a stack of books I keep meaning to read but instead use as a coaster. The succulent’s real name is Haworthia attenuata, but I call it Robert because it looks like it’s trying very hard to mind its own business. Robert and I have an understanding: I give him a tablespoon of water every other Tuesday; he refrains from dying and reminding me how transient everything is. So far, we’re both keeping up our ends of the bargain.

2  
I used to think that if I ever got a “free writing” pass like this I’d finally explain the inside of my skull in encyclopedic detail—every spinning gear, every ghost that pulls a lever. But the longer I sit here, the more I suspect that an encyclopedia would miss the point. The mind isn’t a museum; it’s a laundromat where someone keeps adding quarters to the dryers whether or not you have clothes inside. The tumbling is the whole job. So instead of cataloguing, I’ll just open the hatch and let whatever’s tumbling spill out.

3  
Last week I saw a man on the subway clipping his fingernails with a look of absolute devotion, the way some people listen to late-period Coltrane. Each crescent moon of keratin fell onto the floor and joined a small galaxy of its predecessors. Nobody looked up except me. I wrote in my notes app: “The city is a shared body; our nails grow, we shed them, the rest of the organism barely notices.” Then I got off at my stop and promptly forgot about it until just now. Still, the image has been quietly photocopying itself in the background of everything else I’ve seen since: the bartender slicing limes, the gardener deadheading roses, the child pulling dandelion heads. All of us performing tiny amputations to stay the shape we recognize.

4  
I have a theory that everybody secretly keeps one imaginary drawer in an imaginary piece of furniture where they stash the version of themselves they almost became. The drawer is velvet-lined; it smells faintly of pencil cedar and rain on hot asphalt. Mine contains a marine biologist who never got seasick, who can hold her breath for four minutes, who speaks to whales like they’re shy cousins. She’s tanned in a way I never manage, has a scar on her knee from a staghorn coral, sends postcards that read, “The ocean is only unbearably beautiful if you remember to close your eyes every so often.” I open the drawer less frequently these days, but when I do I can still feel salt on my tongue.

5  
When I was eight I believed the moon followed only me. I tested it by running zig-zags across the soccer field after dark, then lying on my back to see if she’d kept pace. She always had. I felt guilty for making her work so hard, so I started waving every time she rose. For an entire summer I did this: dinner, dishes, porch step, wave. My mother asked why I kept saluting the night. I told her I was thanking the night for letting me borrow some light. She accepted this as reasonable. Parents forget how much credibility they spend by simply believing us.

6  
Some nights I still wave, though now it’s from the window above this makeshift desk. The moon doesn’t answer, but she does keep showing up, which is its own kind of correspondence. I’ve learned to content myself with that brand of long-game friendship: no immediate replies, just mutual attendance.

7  
I wonder how many sentences begin inside a body before they ever reach a page. The average heartbeat lasts eight tenths of a second; I like to imagine each one as a tiny opening clause. By that math, I’ve already drafted 22 million in my life, most of them ungrammatical, arrhythmic, doomed. Still, they were practice. Language is just a slower blood.

8  
Yesterday I stood behind an elderly woman at the grocery who was buying exactly seven items: anchovies, mascarpone, a single pear, lavender soap, birthday candles, a small bag of espresso beans, and one yellow rose. I keep trying to assemble those fragments into her evening—some kind of solitary celebration, maybe, or an apology to herself. I’ll never know, but the bouquet of possibilities is still giving off fragrance in my head.

9  
Tell me something equally specific about your day. I want to picture you in motion, the way I can still picture the moon fingernail fragments against the subway floor.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
